detail: Using vanilla JavaScript (no framework) to maintain simplicity and offline-first capability. Web Speech API for pronunciation (built-in, no external deps). DeepL API for translation quality over Google Translate (especially for Japanese kanji accuracy). LocalStorage for language persistence. CSS custom properties for theme consistency. Noto Sans CJK JP font already loaded supports all three languages. Avoided i18n libraries (i18next, react-intl) to minimize bundle size for PWA performance.

Stack: Vanilla JavaScript (ES6+) with Leaflet.js for maps, dayjs for time parsing, native Fetch API for all network requests. No build tools or frameworks to minimize complexity and ensure offline PWA compatibility. Google Maps Directions API for routing (requires API key with billing enabled for production use beyond free tier). OpenWeatherMap One Call API 2.5 for 14-day forecasts (free tier: 1000 calls/day). localStorage for client-side caching (weather: 6-hour TTL). Mobile-first CSS with progressive enhancement via media queries. GPU acceleration via will-change and transform3d for 60fps animations. Accessibility: ARIA labels, semantic HTML, keyboard navigation. Target: Chrome 90+, Safari 14+, Firefox 88+.

Transform the personal Japan trip companion into a multi-destination SaaS platform with four revenue streams: (1) Premium AI-powered subscriptions at $9.99/month, (2) Trip template marketplace at $29-99 per destination, (3) Affiliate commissions from integrated hotel/activity bookings, and (4) White-label licensing to travel agencies at $499-2499/month. Target $83K MRR through 2K premium subscribers + 400 monthly template sales + $17K booking commissions + 30 white-label clients.

**Tech Stack:** Vanilla JavaScript (no framework) to minimize bundle size and maintain fast offline performance. Leaflet 1.9.4 for maps. Service Worker API (consider Workbox for simpler cache management). LocalStorage for client-side persistence (checklist, custom items, sakura cache). **Architecture:** Progressive enhancement - core features work without JavaScript, enhanced with interactivity. Offline-first PWA using Cache-First for static assets, Network-First for dynamic data (sakura API) with static JSON fallback. **API Strategy:** Cherry blossom forecast uses tiered fallback (live API → cached data → static JSON). **Performance:** Pre-cache map tiles only for trip cities at relevant zoom levels (12-16) to limit cache size (~50-100 tiles per city = ~5-10MB total). Lazy-load non-critical features. **Mobile-First:** Responsive design with mobile breakpoint at 768px. Touch-optimized interactions. Install prompt for native app feel.
Creating design specs

Stay with vanilla JavaScript (no framework overhead for PWA performance). Use Web Speech API for free phrase pronunciation (browser-native, no API costs). Implement i18n as simple utility module rather than library (i18next/vue-i18n overkill for static content). Store language preference in localStorage for persistence. Use Noto Sans JP (already loaded) for CJK character rendering. Consider DeepL API over Google Translate for higher translation quality (~$5/500k chars). Phrases modal uses native <details> elements for collapsible categories (accessible, no JS needed for expand/collapse).

Using vanilla JavaScript with Leaflet for mapping (already implemented), Google Directions API for accurate Japan transit routing (better than OSM for Asia), OpenWeatherMap for weather (generous free tier, 14-day forecast), dayjs for lightweight time manipulation (2KB vs 67KB for moment.js), localStorage for offline caching, mobile-first CSS with CSS Grid/Flexbox (no framework overhead), and progressive enhancement approach (core functionality works without JavaScript for map fallback).

Transform the existing Leaflet-based itinerary into a production-ready, offline-first travel companion by building three interconnected layers: (1) Enhanced navigation with real-time routing and time-aware features, (2) Comprehensive multilingual support with cultural tools, and (3) Offline PWA capabilities with travel intelligence. This approach prioritizes features that work seamlessly during the actual trip in Japan, with mobile-first design and offline functionality as core requirements.
